steps.title: Identify the key signal
steps.content: The passage notes that “Nice distinctions are troublesome. It is so much easier to make up your mind that your neighbour is good for nothing, than to enter into all the circumstances that would oblige you to modify that opinion.” This highlights the human tendency to judge hastily.

steps.title: Match the signal to the purpose
steps.content:
A directly addresses the passage’s point that people readily label neighbours as worthless without examining nuances, which explains the Milby residents’ view of the Countess.
B, C, and D either propose improving opinion, justify reproach, or seek compassion—none of which the passage does.
That matches choice A — A is correct.
